---
title: "A grammar of fisheries data"
---

## Motivation

In 2010, Hadley Wickham formalised the **grammar of graphics** in R through
ggplot2 [@wickham2010]. The central insight was not aesthetic but structural:
any statistical graphic, regardless of type or complexity, can be decomposed
into a small set of composable primitives — data, aesthetic mappings, geometric
objects, scales, facets, and a coordinate system. Give those primitives
consistent names and rules, and you can describe every plot in the same
language.

Fisheries data has an analogous problem. Every country, every regulatory body,
every research institute that collects logbook or survey data has its own
schema. Column names differ (`visir`, `station_id`, `veidi_id`, `VE_REF`,
`LE_CDAT`). Coordinate encodings differ: DDMMss integers, DDM floats, decimal
degrees, ICES rectangles (~50 km × ~50 km). Gear code vocabularies differ.
Time representations differ. Catch is sometimes long (one row per species),
sometimes wide (one column per species). Yet the underlying reality being
recorded is always the same: *a vessel leaves port, deploys fishing gear one
or more times, catches fish, and returns to port.*

But naming inconsistency is only half the problem. The deeper issue is one of
**resolution**: some systems record individual fishing operations with precise
GPS positions and gear deployment timestamps; others aggregate many such
operations into a single daily entry attributed to an area the size of a
county. The same four-level hierarchy underlies every system — the difference
is how finely each level is resolved.

This document proposes a minimal, unambiguous table structure — a grammar —
that can represent any fisheries observation, commercial or scientific,
regardless of its country of origin or degree of spatial and temporal
resolution. The Icelandic logbook project and the EU/ICES VMS and logbook
data-call serve as complementary test cases: the former is high-resolution
(individual fishing operations, GPS positions); the latter is low-resolution
(aggregate daily events, ICES rectangle attribution).

## The hierarchy

Fishing activity is naturally hierarchical. Four levels cover essentially all
cases:

```
Trip
 └── Station  (one or more per trip)
      └── Sample  (one or more per station)
           └── Catch / Measurement  (one or more per sample)
```

The relationship is strictly one-to-many at each level: one trip may contain
many stations; one station may contain many samples; one sample may contain
many individual catch or measurement records. Foreign keys flow downward only;
each child record carries the identifier of its parent.

An important observation is that there is no fundamental difference between a
commercial fishing trip and a scientific survey trip. Both consist of a vessel
making a voyage during which gear is deployed at discrete locations. The grammar
accommodates both; the difference lies in the *richness* of the sample and
measurement tables, not in the structure of the trip and station tables.

## Level 1 — Trip

A trip is the unit of a single voyage: departure from a harbour, operations at
sea, and return to a harbour. It is the administrative unit of fisheries
management (vessel licence, landing declaration, crew count).

**Essential columns:**

| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `.tid` | int / chr | Trip identifier (primary key) |
| `vid` | int / chr | Vessel identifier |
| `T1` | datetime | Departure time |
| `hid1` | int / chr | Departure harbour |
| `T2` | datetime | Landing / return time |
| `hid2` | int / chr | Landing harbour |

The `.tid` and `.sid` identifiers use a dot prefix throughout this project to
signal that they are *synthetic* keys — either assigned by the source system
or derived during processing — as opposed to natural domain keys like `vid`
(vessel number) or `sid` (species code), which have a stable external
definition. Uppercase `T1`/`T2` denote trip-level times to distinguish them
visually from station-level times `t0`/`t1`/`t2`.

A trip record can legitimately exist with no associated station records: a
vessel may turn back due to weather, move between harbours in ballast, or
simply have no detailed operation records in the source data. The grammar
handles this naturally — the station table is a child of the trip table, not
the other way around.

In some source systems no explicit trip table exists and must be derived.
In the Icelandic `afli` legacy schema, trips are reconstructed by grouping
stations on the same vessel sharing the same anticipated landing date and
harbour, with `.tid` assigned as the minimum `.sid` within each group.
In the EU eflalo format, the trip is likewise embedded in the logbook record
(`FT_DDAT`/`FT_DTIME`, `FT_LDAT`/`FT_LTIME`) and must be extracted and
de-duplicated: every row belonging to the same trip carries the same departure
and landing times, so `.tid` is derived via `consecutive_id()` on those columns.

## Level 2 — Station

A station is a discrete fishing operation: the deployment and retrieval of gear
at a particular place and time. It is where the scientific interest is
concentrated — effort, position, timing, and gear configuration are all
station-level attributes.

**Essential columns:**

| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `.sid` | int | Station identifier (primary key) |
| `.tid` | int / chr | Parent trip (foreign key) |
| `gid` | int / chr | Gear type code |
| `date` | date | Fishing date |
| `t0` | datetime | Gear deployment (static gears) |
| `t1` | datetime | Start of haul / tow |
| `t2` | datetime | End of haul / tow |
| `lon1`, `lat1` | dbl | Start position (decimal degrees) |
| `lon2`, `lat2` | dbl | End position (decimal degrees) |
| `z1`, `z2` | dbl | Start / end depth (m) |
| `sq`, `ssq` | int | Statistical square / sub-square |
| `effort` | dbl | Standardised effort value |
| `effort_unit` | chr | Effort unit |

The three time columns `t0`, `t1`, `t2` are not all populated for every gear
type, and their meaning differs between mobile and static gears. This
asymmetry is inherent to the physics of the gear classes, not a schema
deficiency; it is explained in detail in the section below.

Gear-specific effort quantities (sweeps length, number of hooks, number of
nets, trap count) are additive columns on the station table, populated only
for the relevant gear class and `NA` elsewhere. They are not promoted to
separate tables in the unified schema because they are single scalar values
per station; the gear sub-tables in the source systems (`toga`, `lineha`,
`ws_dragnot_varpa`, `ws_linanethandf`, etc.) exist for normalisation in the
originating relational databases, but a flattened station table is more
practical for analysis.

## The time structure of a fishing operation

A fishing operation is a continuous physical process — gear enters the water,
fish are caught, gear is retrieved — yet it is recorded as a *discrete* event
with a small number of timestamps. Which timestamps are meaningful, and which
can be non-overlapping across operations on the same vessel, depends entirely
on the gear class.

### Mobile gear

For mobile gears (bottom trawl, midwater trawl, seine, dredge), one operation
must end before the next can begin: the vessel must retrieve its gear before
redeploying. Operations are therefore strictly sequential. The full time
structure of a single operation has four events:

| Event | Logbook column | Recorded? |
|---|---|---|
| Gear deployment starts (gear enters water) | — | No |
| **Fishing starts** (gear on bottom / tow begins) | **`t1`** | Yes |
| **Fishing ends** (gear lifted / tow ends) | **`t2`** | Yes |
| Gear fully retrieved (gear back on deck) | — | No |

The deployment and retrieval intervals bracketing `t1` and `t2` are short
relative to tow duration and are not recorded in practice. What matters for
analysis is `t2 − t1`: the tow time, which is the primary effort unit for
mobile gears and the basis for swept-area calculations.

The key non-overlap constraint is simple: no two operations on the same vessel
can have overlapping `[t1, t2]` intervals. A vessel can only tow one set of
gear at a time, so any two operations where `t1`/`t2` windows overlap is a
data error.

### Static gear

Static gears (longline, gillnet, trap) operate fundamentally differently. Once
deployed, gear fishes autonomously for hours or days while the vessel is free
to move elsewhere. A vessel can therefore have several sets of gear in the
water simultaneously — gear A soaking while gear B is being deployed, gear C
being retrieved while gear A continues to soak. The outer `[t0, t2]` windows
of concurrent operations legitimately overlap.

The full time structure of a single static-gear operation has five events
(using the grammar labels introduced in the next subsection):

| Event | Grammar label | Recorded? |
|---|---|---|
| **Gear deployment starts** (first hook / net in water) | **`t0`** | Yes |
| Gear deployment ends (last hook / net in water) | `t1` | Rarely |
| [Soak period — gear fishing autonomously] | — | — |
| **Gear retrieval starts** (first hook / net hauled) | **`t2`** | Yes |
| **Gear retrieval ends** (last hook / net on board) | **`t3`** | Yes |

The deployment-end timestamp `t1` is almost never recorded; the deployment
phase is treated as instantaneous in most logbook systems. This is an
acknowledged gap: for a vessel setting 2,000 hooks at 1 knot, the deployment
alone may take two hours, and the unrecorded `t1` can differ substantially
from `t0`.

The non-overlap constraints for static gear are more subtle than for mobile
gear. Two constraints hold that cannot be violated physically:

- **Deployment windows** `[t0, t1]` cannot overlap across concurrent
  operations — a vessel can only be in one place setting gear at a time.
- **Retrieval windows** `[t2, t3]` cannot overlap across concurrent
  operations — a vessel can only be hauling one set of gear at a time.

But the *outer* interval `[t0, t3]` of two operations on the same vessel *can*
overlap legitimately: deploying a new longline while an older one soaks is
normal practice. The timeline below illustrates a typical two-operation
sequence:

```
Operation A:  t0_A ── t1_A · · · · · · · · · t2_A ── t3_A
Operation B:               t0_B ── t1_B · · · · · · · · · t2_B ── t3_B
```

Here operation B's deployment begins after A's deployment ends but before A's
retrieval begins. The outer `[t0, t3]` windows overlap; the `[t0, t1]` and
`[t2, t3]` windows do not.

This has a direct consequence for data quality checking. A check that flags
any two operations on the same vessel with overlapping `[t0, t3]` intervals
will produce false positives for static gear. The correct checks are:

- `t0 < t2 < t3` must hold within every individual operation (with `t1`
  between `t0` and `t2` when recorded).
- No two operations on the same vessel share an overlapping `[t2, t3]`
  retrieval window.
- No two operations on the same vessel share an overlapping `[t0, t1]`
  deployment window — but since `t1` is rarely recorded, this check is
  limited in practice to detecting identical `t0` timestamps.

### A unified time convention: t0 – t3

The preceding discussion used labels that differ between gear classes —
`t1`/`t2` for mobile, `t0`/`t2`/`t3` for static. A grammar should not require
consulting the gear type before interpreting a column name. The four physical
events of any fishing operation map cleanly onto a single sequence:

| Label | Event | Mobile gear | Static gear |
|---|---|---|---|
| `t0` | Gear deployment starts | Warp / net enters water | First hook / net enters water |
| `t1` | Gear deployment ends — fishing begins | Gear on bottom, tow begins | Last hook / net in water, soaking begins |
| `t2` | Fishing ends — gear retrieval starts | Gear lifted off bottom | First hook / net hauled |
| `t3` | Gear retrieval ends | Gear back on deck | Last hook / net on board |

Under this convention the effort quantity of primary interest — active fishing
time — is consistently `t2 − t1` regardless of gear class. `t1 − t0` is always
deployment duration; `t3 − t2` is always retrieval duration.

The non-overlap constraints from the previous sections state uniformly for all
gear types: `[t0, t1]` windows cannot overlap across concurrent operations;
`[t2, t3]` windows cannot overlap; `[t0, t3]` outer windows can overlap for
static gear only.

#### What is typically recorded

No data system records all four events for all gear types. The practical
coverage is:

| Label | Mobile gear | Static gear |
|---|---|---|
| `t0` | Rarely | Usually |
| `t1` | Usually | Rarely |
| `t2` | Usually | Usually |
| `t3` | Rarely | Usually |

For mobile gear the effort calculation depends on `t2 − t1` (tow time), so
`t1` and `t2` are the primary events; `t0` and `t3` bracket the tow but are
not needed for effort. For static gear, soak time (`t2 − t1`) is the
theoretically correct effort measure, but since `t1` (deployment end) is
rarely recorded, soak time is commonly approximated as `t2 − t0` — the
interval from first-hook-in to first-hook-hauled — which overestimates true
soak time by the deployment duration.

#### Resetting the Icelandic vocabulary

The Icelandic logbook systems (`afli`, `fs_afladagbok`) use a `t0`/`t1`/`t2`
convention that predates this grammar. The mapping to grammar labels is:

| Icelandic label | Mobile gear meaning | Grammar label | Static gear meaning | Grammar label |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `t0` | — (not used) | — | Deployment starts | `t0` ✓ |
| `t1` | Tow starts | `t1` ✓ | Retrieval starts | `t2` ✗ |
| `t2` | Tow ends | `t2` ✓ | Retrieval ends | `t3` ✗ |

For mobile gear the Icelandic labels happen to coincide with the grammar.
For static gear they do not: the Icelandic `t1` means retrieval starts
(grammar `t2`), and the Icelandic `t2` means retrieval ends (grammar `t3`).
The event grammar calls `t1` (deployment ends) has no Icelandic label because
it is not recorded.

A reset to grammar conventions would require, **for static gear only**:

- Rename current `t1` → `t2` (retrieval starts)
- Rename current `t2` → `t3` (retrieval ends)
- Add `t1` as a new column for deployment end — currently unrecorded and
  therefore `NA` throughout, but named consistently with mobile gear

No changes are needed for mobile gear columns. After the reset, the time
columns carry the same meaning across all gear types and the unified station
table is unambiguous without reference to `gid`.

## Level 3 — Sample

A sample represents a discrete observational event *within* a station. In
commercial logbook data there is typically only one sample per station — the
catch declaration — so the sample level often collapses into the station level
and is not represented as a separate table.

However, a sample-level table becomes necessary the moment more than one type
of observation is made at the same station:

- A scientific survey tow may record both a catch sample (species composition
  and weight) and a series of hydrographic casts (CTD profiles) at the same
  station position. These are two distinct samples.
- Continuous electronic logger data — position, speed, heading, net opening,
  depth — recorded during a tow constitute a third type of sample: a time
  series of sensor readings rather than a single aggregate measurement.

The `afli` legacy system implicitly recognises this: `stofn.parquet` is the
station table, while `rafr_sjalfvirkir_maelar.parquet` is a separate table of
automatic logger readings linked to the same station via `visir` (`.sid`). The
logger table is a sample in the grammar's sense — a different observation type,
at a finer temporal resolution, recorded alongside the main fishing operation.

**Minimal columns for a sample table:**

| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `.smid` | int | Sample identifier (primary key) |
| `.sid` | int | Parent station (foreign key) |
| `sample_type` | chr | Kind of observation (`"catch"`, `"ctd"`, `"logger"`, …) |

## Level 4 — Catch / Measurement

At the lowest level sit the individual observations. For commercial fishing
these are catch records: one row per species per station (or per sample, if the
sample level is present). For scientific surveys they may be individual fish
measurements (length, weight, age, sex).

**Catch table (commercial):**

| Column | Type | Description |
|--------|------|-------------|
| `.sid` | int | Parent station (foreign key) |
| `sid` | int / chr | Species identifier |
| `catch` | dbl | Landed weight (kg) |

Note the deliberate naming distinction: `.sid` (dot-prefixed) is the station
synthetic key; `sid` (no dot) is the species code — a stable external
identifier from the species vocabulary. The two happen to share the stem "sid"
for different reasons, and the dot prefix is the only disambiguation needed.

## The column vocabulary

The grammar is only as useful as its naming conventions are consistent. The
table below lists the core column names; collectively they form a controlled
vocabulary that any downstream script can rely on without inspecting the source
schema.

| Name | Level | Meaning |
|------|-------|---------|
| `.tid` | Trip | Synthetic trip key |
| `.sid` | Station | Synthetic station key |
| `vid` | Trip | Vessel identifier |
| `cid` | Trip | Country identifier |
| `T1`, `T2` | Trip | Departure / landing time (uppercase = trip level) |
| `hid1`, `hid2` | Trip | Departure / landing harbour |
| `n_crew` | Trip | Crew count |
| `gid` | Station | Gear type code |
| `date` | Station | Fishing date |
| `t0`, `t1`, `t2` | Station | Gear deployment / haul start / haul end (lowercase = station level) |
| `lon1`, `lat1` | Station | Start position |
| `lon2`, `lat2` | Station | End position |
| `z1`, `z2` | Station | Start / end depth |
| `sq`, `ssq` | Station | Statistical square / sub-square |
| `effort`, `effort_unit` | Station | Standardised effort |
| `towtime` | Station | Tow duration (minutes) |
| `gear_width` | Station | Effective gear width |
| `sid` | Catch | Species identifier |
| `catch` | Catch | Catch weight (kg) |

Three naming conventions are worth making explicit:

- **Dot-prefix** on `.tid`, `.sid`, `.smid` marks synthetic or derived keys,
  not natural domain identifiers. Columns generated inside processing functions
  — and not present in the raw input — also carry a dot prefix (e.g. `.intv`,
  `.checks`).
- **Uppercase / lowercase** separates trip-level times (`T1`, `T2`) from
  station-level times (`t0`, `t1`, `t2`). This avoids name collisions and
  makes the temporal level of a column unambiguous at a glance.
- **Numeric suffix** on paired columns (`lon1`/`lon2`, `lat1`/`lat2`,
  `z1`/`z2`, `hid1`/`hid2`) consistently encodes start (1) and end (2),
  regardless of which physical direction "start" implies for a given gear type.

## Resolution and the hierarchy

Not all data systems resolve the hierarchy to the same depth. The grammar
describes the ideal; real-world data falls somewhere along a resolution
continuum from individual fishing operation to aggregate daily summary.

The table below compares the Icelandic logbook system with the EU/ICES
eflalo format, which is the standard for the annual ICES VMS and logbook
data-call submitted by EU member states:

| | Icelandic (`afli` / `fs_afladagbok`) | EU eflalo |
|-|-------------------------------------|-----------|
| **Trip** | Explicit record (`ws_veidiferd`) or derived from station grouping | Derived: repeated `FT_DDAT` + `FT_LDAT` fields on every logbook row |
| **Station** | Individual fishing operation; GPS start/end; deployment timestamps | Absent as an explicit concept — replaced by an *event*: trip × gear × day × ICES rectangle |
| **Spatial resolution** | GPS position (metres) | ICES statistical rectangle (~50 km × ~50 km) |
| **Temporal resolution** | Gear deployment and retrieval times (minutes) | Calendar date only (`LE_CDAT`); `t1`/`t2` present only when `LE_STIME`/`LE_ETIME` reported |
| **Catch format** | Long (one row per species per station) | Wide (one column per species: `LE_KG_COD`, `LE_KG_HAD`, …) |
| **Effort** | Derived from gear timestamps and gear-specific units | Not explicitly recorded; must be inferred from trip duration and gear type |
| **Position data source** | Logbook (GPS recorded by skipper or electronic system) | VMS / AIS pings required for spatial attribution |

The EU "event" deserves particular attention. What appears in an eflalo row
as a single observation — a gear deployed on a given date in a given rectangle
— may in reality represent several distinct fishing operations. A trawler
working the same grounds all day in the same ICES rectangle produces one eflalo
row regardless of whether it made two hauls or ten. The station level is simply
not recorded; only the aggregate is.

This is not a deficiency unique to EU reporting: many national logbook systems
operate at the same resolution, and even some Icelandic vessels in the early
`afli` period reported only date and rectangle without timestamps or GPS. The
eflalo format merely formalises this aggregate reporting as a standard.

The wide catch format is a separate structural choice with practical
consequences: pivoting from `LE_KG_<SP>` columns to long format (one row
per species) is a required preprocessing step before any species-level
analysis, and the number of species columns varies across submissions and
years, making automated processing fragile.

## Reconstructing station resolution from position data

When logbook data only records events (trip × gear × day × rectangle), station-
level spatial and temporal resolution can be partially recovered by linking
logbook records to high-frequency vessel position data — either VMS (Vessel
Monitoring System) pings, transmitted at 2-hour intervals, or AIS
(Automatic Identification System) pings, available at much higher frequency
(minutes or seconds for many vessels).

The reconstruction proceeds in two steps:

**Step 1 — Activity classification.** Not every ping represents active fishing;
vessels also steam between grounds, enter harbour, drift at anchor, and shelter
from weather. Fishing activity is inferred from vessel speed: a trawler moving
at 2–4 knots is likely towing gear; the same vessel at 10 knots is steaming.
Speed thresholds by gear type are estimated from the bimodal speed distribution
of fishing versus steaming, typically fit using a Gaussian mixture model
[@woillez2016]. Pings classified as "fishing" become implicit station records
at the temporal and spatial resolution of the position data.

**Step 2 — Catch distribution.** Logbook catch, recorded at the event level
(trip × gear × day × rectangle), must be distributed across the individual
fishing pings that fall within that event. The simplest approach assigns equal
catch weight to each ping; more sophisticated methods weight by swept area,
gear width, or tow duration.

This two-step procedure is the foundation of the ICES VMS and logbook data-call
workflow, implemented in the `osfd` R package [@osfd]. The key structural point
is that the procedure is a *consequence* of the station level being absent from
the logbook data: were individual fishing operations recorded with timestamps
and GPS positions — as they are in the Icelandic high-resolution system — no
catch distribution step would be needed. The catch would already be attributed
to a precise time and place.

The high-resolution Icelandic data illustrates what the EU data is trying to
approximate. A bottom trawler with full electronic logbook coverage has `t1`,
`t2`, `lon1`/`lat1`, `lon2`/`lat2` for every haul: the station table is
complete. The same vessel operating under EU reporting requirements would
produce a single eflalo row for the day, and a reconstructed station table
derived from VMS pings would be the best available substitute. The grammar
is the same; the provenance of the station records differs.

VMS and AIS pings themselves form a natural sample-level table in the grammar:
they are repeated positional observations associated with a station (fishing
event), at finer temporal resolution than the logbook entry. In the `osfd`
workflow, processed pings carry `.pid` (ping identifier), `vid`, `time`, `lon`,
`lat`, `speed`, and `heading` — a table that maps directly onto the sample
level of the hierarchy, with the classified fishing pings serving as implicit
station boundaries.

## How data systems map onto the grammar

| | `afli` | `fs_afladagbok` | `adb` | EU eflalo |
|-|--------|-----------------|-------|-----------|
| **Trip key** | Derived: `min(.sid)` per `(vid, T2, hid2)` | `ws_veidiferd.id` | `trip_v.trip_id` | Derived: `consecutive_id()` on `FT_DDAT`/`FT_LDAT` |
| **Station key** | `stofn.visir` | `ws_veidi.id` | `station_v.station_id` | `.eid` (row number — not a true station) |
| **Station concept** | Individual fishing operation | Individual fishing operation | Individual fishing operation | Aggregate: trip × gear × day × rectangle |
| **Gear code** | Legacy integer (old) | New integer (canonical) | New integer (derived) | ICES metier string (e.g. `OTB`, `LLS`) |
| **Spatial resolution** | GPS (metres) | GPS (metres) | GPS (variable quality) | ICES rectangle (~50 km) |
| **Temporal resolution** | Minutes (on-bottom clock) | Minutes (electronic timestamps) | Minutes (electronic timestamps) | Day (`LE_CDAT`); rarely `t1`/`t2` |
| **Catch format** | Long | Long | Long | Wide (`LE_KG_<SP>` columns) |

The gear code unification for the Icelandic schemas is handled by
`gear_mapping` (`data/gear/gear_mapping.parquet`), which maps the legacy many-
to-one from old codes to new. For the EU eflalo system, gear is expressed as
an ICES metier string (`LE_GEAR`, `LE_MET`) that encodes gear type, target
species, and mesh size, and maps onto the same ICES controlled vocabulary via
`sfdSAR::predict_gear_width()` for swept-area calculations.

## Scope and extensions

The grammar described here is intentionally minimal. The four levels and the
column vocabulary above are sufficient to represent commercial logbook data
across all gear types and reporting resolutions encountered in practice — from
the full-detail Icelandic system to the aggregate EU eflalo format. The grammar
also accommodates scientific survey data with minor additions at the sample and
measurement levels (sample type, individual fish measurements, hydrographic
observations).

What the grammar does *not* prescribe is the encoding of controlled
vocabularies: which codes are valid gear types, which harbour identifiers are
valid, which species codes are in use. Those are lookup tables —
`gear_mapping`, the harbour register, the species list, the ICES metier
vocabulary — that sit alongside the grammar tables but are not part of the
core hierarchy. Keeping them separate means the grammar itself is stable even
as vocabularies evolve.

Two extensions are of immediate practical interest:

1. **Sample table for logger data.** The automatic logger records in `afli`
   (`rafr_sjalfvirkir_maelar`) currently exist as a parallel table linked by
   `.sid` but without a formal sample identifier. Introducing `.smid` would
   allow multiple logger sessions per station to be cleanly represented and
   would make the hierarchy fully consistent. The same structure would cover
   VMS / AIS ping tables once they are linked to individual station records.

2. **Degraded-station flag.** A column `station_type` (or similar) on the
   station table could record whether a station record represents a genuine
   individual fishing operation (`"operation"`) or an aggregate logbook event
   reconstructed from VMS pings (`"event"`, `"reconstructed"`). This makes
   the provenance of spatial attribution explicit and allows downstream
   analyses to apply appropriate caveats.
